macro_segments.title: Introduction to Perek Two: The Five Categories of Chatat Meita and Textual Issues

micro_segments.title: Overview of Chatat Meita: The three clear cases and the two conditional cases
The distinction between absolute cases (vlad, temura, meitu ba'aleha) and conditional cases (avar shnata, kiperu ba'alim ba'acher)
Analysis of the manuscript reading: mishikipru vs kipru ba'alim and its implications for the status of a chatat meita
The implications of chatat meita: inability to perform temura, inability to benefit from it, and exemption from me'ila

macro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's Position and the Double Problem Requirement for Avar Shnata

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's statement that chatat over a year old is viewed as in a cemetery, not requiring death
The contradiction from the Mishna: combining avar shnata with being lost and having a mum creates a case of chatat meita
Reish Lakish's response: the final section applies only to lost animals with a mum, not to avar shnata alone
Rava's attempt to reconcile the text by distinguishing between temporary and permanent mum
The fundamental question: what is the halacha for avar shnata, and why does the Mishna include it?

macro_segments.title: The Required Combination of Problems: Lost Plus an Additional Problem

micro_segments.title: The gemara's explanation for why both avar shnata and ba'alat mum must be listed separately
The principle that avar shnata alone is not enough; it requires combination with being lost
The case of ba'alat mum: can be brought for other offerings but not this one, requiring the double problem
The distinction between essential and accidental defects and their role in determining chatat meita status

macro_segments.title: The Case of Avida De'laila (Lost at Night) and Relevance to the Offering

micro_segments.title: Raba's statement that loss at night does not constitute true loss
Application to Rabbi and Chachamim: different understandings of when loss becomes definitive
The distinction between loss at daytime versus nighttime and the status at the time of atonement
The principle that irrelevance to the zman hakrava (time of offering) affects the status of loss

macro_segments.title: Defining Avida (Loss): What Constitutes a Lost Animal

micro_segments.title: Abye's conclusion regarding what counts as loss: lost not stolen, lost not robbed
Rabbi Yoshia's standards for loss: even one among many, even indistinguishable from another
Rabbi Yochanan's statement about loss: behind the door is considered lost
The debate whether loss requires complete invisibility or only unknowability of location

macro_segments.title: Complex Cases: Loss Involving Blood in Vessels and the Status of Multiple Vessels

micro_segments.title: The scenario of loss occurring after shechita, when blood is caught in a vessel
The question of whether blood in a vessel becomes an offering when the animal itself is found
The distinction between blood in one vessel versus multiple vessels and the status of rejected blood
The debate whether kos oseh chaveiro dichui (one vessel rejects another) applies to missing blood

macro_segments.title: The Mishna: The Two Positions of Rabbi and Chachamim on When Chatat Meita Applies

micro_segments.title: Universal agreement: when a chatat is lost and replaced and found after the replacement is brought
The case of lost money replaced by other money and found before purchase of a new chatat
The principle of mixing money together to avoid creating a chatat meita through rejection
Cases involving both animals and money: redeeming a blemished animal to create fungible money
The central dispute: Rabbi says even finding before atonement creates chatat meita; Chachamim say only after
The special case of Rabbi Elazar bar Rabbi Shimon: selling a blemished chatat creates meita status for the buyer's animal
